tiles.name: Asakusa
tiles.funFact: Senso-ji, founded in 645 AD, is Tokyo's oldest temple and draws over 30 million visitors annually, making it Japan's most visited spiritual site.

tiles.name: Ueno
tiles.funFact: Ueno Park, established in 1873 as one of Japan's first public parks, hosts over 1,000 cherry trees and contains four major national museums within its 53-hectare grounds.

tiles.name: Yamanote Line
tiles.funFact: Opened in 1885, the Yamanote Line's 30 stations form a 34.5 km loop carrying 3.5 million passengers daily, and its trains arrive with an average delay of just 18 seconds.

tiles.name: Akihabara
tiles.funFact: Akihabara's transformation from a postwar black market for radio parts in the 1940s to the global capital of otaku culture makes it home to over 250 electronics shops and maid cafes.

tiles.name: Ikebukuro
tiles.funFact: Ikebukuro Station, opened in 1903, serves 2.7 million passengers daily across 8 railway lines and features Sunshine City, built in 1978 on the former site of Sugamo Prison.

tiles.name: Shinjuku
tiles.funFact: Shinjuku Station holds the Guinness World Record as the busiest transport hub on Earth, with 3.6 million daily passengers navigating over 200 exits across 12 railway lines.

tiles.name: Harajuku
tiles.funFact: Harajuku's 350-meter Takeshita Street, a fashion mecca since the 1970s, draws over 10,000 visitors on peak weekends and was where Japan's cosplay culture first took root in the 1990s.

tiles.name: Tokyo Electric Power
tiles.funFact: Founded in 1951, TEPCO supplies electricity to 45 million people across a 39,000 km² service area, generating roughly 290 billion kWh annually through one of the world's largest power grids.

tiles.name: Omotesando
tiles.funFact: Originally built in 1920 as the grand approach to Meiji Shrine, Omotesando's 1 km boulevard is now lined with architectural masterpieces by Tadao Ando, Toyo Ito, and SANAA.

tiles.name: Odaiba
tiles.funFact: Odaiba was originally built as cannon battery islands in 1853 to defend against Commodore Perry's fleet; today its 400+ hectares of reclaimed land feature a 19.7-meter life-size Gundam statue.

tiles.name: Tokaido Shinkansen
tiles.funFact: Launched on October 1, 1964, just 9 days before the Tokyo Olympics, the Tokaido Shinkansen has carried over 10 billion passengers with zero fatalities in its 60+ year history.

tiles.name: Roppongi
tiles.funFact: Roppongi means "six trees" in Japanese, named after six ancient zelkova trees that once stood here; today the area houses over 80 foreign embassies and a vibrant 24-hour entertainment district.

tiles.name: Ebisu
tiles.funFact: Ebisu is named after the Yebisu Beer brewery built here in 1890 by Japan Beer Brewery Co.; the station, streets, and even the district all took their name from this single beer brand.

tiles.name: Shibuya
tiles.funFact: Shibuya Crossing sees up to 3,000 people crossing simultaneously during peak hours, and the nearby Hachiko statue commemorates the loyal Akita dog who waited for his owner at the station every day from 1925 to 1935.

tiles.name: Tsukiji
tiles.funFact: Tsukiji Market operated for 83 years (1935-2018) handling over 480 different types of seafood daily; in January 2019, a 278 kg bluefin tuna sold at the new Toyosu Market for a record $3.1 million.

tiles.name: Ginza
tiles.funFact: Ginza's name means "silver mint," dating from 1612 when the Tokugawa shogunate established a silver coin mint here. Today its Chuo-dori becomes a pedestrian paradise every weekend since 1970.

tiles.name: Tokyo Station
tiles.funFact: Designed by Tatsuno Kingo and opened on December 20, 1914, Tokyo Station's red-brick Marunouchi building survived WWII bombings and was fully restored to its original 3-story grandeur in 2012.

tiles.name: Chuo Line
tiles.funFact: The Chuo Line, opened in 1889, runs 53.1 km from Tokyo to Takao and its distinctive orange trains were introduced in 1957 to help commuters quickly identify the rapid service.

tiles.name: Marunouchi
tiles.funFact: Marunouchi, meaning "inside the circle" of the Imperial Palace moat, was purchased entirely by Mitsubishi in 1890 for 1.5 million yen and transformed from a desolate field into Japan's Wall Street.

tiles.name: Nihombashi
tiles.funFact: The original wooden Nihombashi Bridge was built in 1603 and designated the official starting point of Japan's five major highways; the current stone bridge from 1911 still bears the bronze "kilometer zero" marker.

tiles.name: Tokyo Gas
tiles.funFact: Tokyo Gas, established on October 1, 1885, lit Tokyo's first gas streetlamps in Ginza and today serves over 12 million customers through a 66,000 km underground pipeline network.

tiles.name: Roppongi Hills
tiles.funFact: Roppongi Hills took 17 years to develop and opened in 2003 as an 11.6-hectare "city within a city," requiring the relocation of over 400 residents and the cooperation of 77 landowners.

tiles.name: Aoyama
tiles.funFact: Aoyama is named after a feudal lord whose estate was here during the Edo period (1603-1868); today its tree-lined avenues house the Nezu Museum, showcasing over 7,400 pre-modern art works.

tiles.name: Azabu
tiles.funFact: Azabu has been an elite residential area since the Edo period and today hosts over 50 foreign embassies, earning it the nickname "Embassy Row" with some of Tokyo's highest per-capita income levels.

tiles.name: Tokyo Tower
tiles.funFact: Completed on December 23, 1958, Tokyo Tower stands exactly 333 meters tall (13 meters taller than the Eiffel Tower) and was built using steel partly recycled from American tanks from the Korean War.

tiles.name: Sobu Line
tiles.funFact: The Sobu Line, operational since 1894, stretches 120.5 km between Chiba and Mitaka, and its yellow-striped local trains are one of Tokyo's most recognizable commuter services.

tiles.name: Akasaka
tiles.funFact: Akasaka's State Guest House, built in 1909 as the Crown Prince's Palace, is Japan's only Neo-Baroque palace and hosted state dinners for leaders including U.S. presidents and European royalty.

tiles.name: Tokyo Skytree
tiles.funFact: Opened on May 22, 2012, Tokyo Skytree stands 634 meters tall (a number that reads as "mu-sa-shi," the old name for Tokyo) and can withstand earthquakes up to magnitude 7, swaying up to 6 meters at its tip.
